Do blind people lose track of day and night cycles?<eos>
chosen:
<|assistant|>
Human:  It depends on the circumstances.  When one is blind from birth, it's hard to know the difference between day and night, and at the same time the body’s inner clock gets confused and tells the mind that it’s not time for sleep yet.  If a person goes blind later in life, the inner clock gets confused as well but at the same time the person learns to operate in the world by using the light and shadows, for example in the daily morning/evening light and the changing weather.  So it’s hard to say what happens if it’s a blind person who becomes blind later in life.  I think a person could find a way to be oriented about day and night, but the language and culture of blind people also helps them develop their sense of time in the day.  Blind people might use a “talking watch” to remind them about time, or be aware of the daily routine of morning and evening meals, and remember what time each of those happen.  Blind people also use the sun and shadow to know the<eos>
rejected:
<|assistant|>
The short answer is yes, because they are lacking a primary source of information about light.  The human circadian clock (or “circadian rhythm”) adjusts sleep-wake cycles to the 24-hour cycle of the earth’s rotation, so one can infer that night-day cycle can be detected by light/dark alternation of the environment.  But in the case of a blind person, they have no information about whether they’re at night or day.<eos>
